title: NA
text: pjrt runtime layer r-xla stack. compiles StableHLO/MLIR programs hardware-specific executables runs via PJRT C API. supports CPU, CUDA, Metal backends dynamically loaded plugins.

title: NA
text: core objects S3 classes backed C++ external pointers (via Rcpp):
code: PJRTPlugin       – loaded shared library (.so/.dylib) providing the PJRT C API   └─ PJRTClient  – owns devices, creates buffers, compiles programs        └─ PJRTDevice  – a single device (e.g. "cpu:0", "cuda:1") PJRTProgram            – MLIR or HLO source code ready for compilation   └─ PJRTLoadedExecutable  – compiled, device-specific executable PJRTBuffer         – device-resident tensor (the main data object) PJRTArrayPromise   – async device-to-host transfer result (environment-based) PJRTElementType    – dtype enum (pred, i8–i64, ui8–ui64, f32, f64)

title: NA
text: Plugins clients singletons cached global environment (plugin.R): pjrt_client(platform) checks cache, otherwise loads plugin creates client. Plugin loading: checks PJRT_PLUGIN_PATH_<PLATFORM> env var, falls back downloading zml/pjrt-artifacts R_user_dir("pjrt", "cache"). One client per platform — calling pjrt_client() returns cached instance.

title: NA
text: typical workflow : create program → compile → create buffers → execute → read results.
code: prog <- pjrt_program(src, format = "mlir")       # PJRTProgram exec <- pjrt_compile(prog, device = "cpu")        # PJRTLoadedExecutable buf  <- pjrt_buffer(data, dtype = "f32")           # PJRTBuffer (host → device) out  <- pjrt_execute(exec, buf)                    # PJRTBuffer (on device, may not be ready) arr  <- as_array(out)                              # R array (device → host, blocks)

title: NA
text: execution buffer transfers asynchronous: pjrt_execute() returns PJRTBuffers may ready yet. PJRT tracks dependencies internally, unready buffers can passed directly inputs next execution. as_array_async() returns PJRTArrayPromise (non-blocking device--host transfer). is_ready() polls without blocking; await() value() block complete.

title: NA
text: R uses column-major (Fortran) order. C++ layer handles row--column-major conversion transferring R device buffers.

title: NA
text: plugin.R – plugin loading, client caching, global state () client.R – pjrt_client(), pjrt_compile() buffer.R – pjrt_buffer(), pjrt_scalar(), pjrt_empty(), type dispatch loaded_executable.R – pjrt_execute() async.R – value(), is_ready(), await(), PJRTArrayPromise device.R – pjrt_device(), device spec parsing (“cpu:0”) program.R – pjrt_program() (MLIR/HLO loading) format.R – buffer pretty-printing safetensors.R – safetensors read/write integration reexports.R – tengen re-exports src/ – Rcpp C++ layer wrapping PJRT C API, plus protobuf compile options Important: call devtools::load_all() devtools::test() R process. protobuf descriptors get registered twice, causing fatal CHECK failed: GeneratedDatabase()->Add(...) crash. Use separate Rscript -e calls instead.

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: {pjrt} ships built-custom calls registered automatically package loaded. includes print_tensor linear-algebra primitives geqrf / orgqr (QR factorisation), lu, svd, eigh. example uses print_tensor, takes single tensor operand, prints , returns nothing. stablehlo.custom_call op carries: call_target_name – string names handler registered PJRT runtime (, built-"print_tensor"). Note name also appears @print_tensor op name textual form; @<name> purely pretty-printer convention mirrors call_target_name, attribute string operative runtime. api_version = 4 : i32 – calling convention used invoke handler. Just set 4. has_side_effect = true – required ops exist side effect (printing). wouldn’t set TRUE , compiler might drop operation one optimization passes. backend_config = {...} (optional) – dictionary attributes handler can read. print_tensor accepts print_header (default "PJRTBuffer") print_footer (default "[ <dtype>{<shape>} ]"); pass "" either suppress line. operand_layouts / result_layouts indicate layout custom call expects outputs (e.g., row-major vs. col-major). XLA’s documented default newly-created shapes row-major (XLA Shapes, ’s best always set clarity. Next, discuss register custom call.
code: library(pjrt)  src <- r"( func.func @main(%x: tensor<2x3xf32>) -> tensor<2x3xf32> {   stablehlo.custom_call @print_tensor(%x) {     call_target_name = "print_tensor",     backend_config = {       print_header = "my matrix",       print_footer = "----"     },     has_side_effect = true,     api_version = 4 : i32,     operand_layouts = [dense<[1, 0]> : tensor<2xindex>],     result_layouts = []   } : (tensor<2x3xf32>) -> ()    "func.return"(%x) : (tensor<2x3xf32>) -> () } )"  exec <- pjrt_compile(pjrt_program(src)) buf  <- pjrt_buffer(matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3), dtype = "f32") out  <- pjrt_execute(exec, buf) ## my matrix ##  1 3 5 ##  2 4 6 ## ---- as_array(out) ##      [,1] [,2] [,3] ## [1,]    1    3    5 ## [2,]    2    4    6

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: order make custom call availble {stablehlo} program, need : Define . Register (one ) PJRTPlugins (CUDA, CPU) can used. Generally, need different implementations different plugins (CPU vs. CUDA). start discussing CPU, simpler.

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: CPU handler just C++ function takes one parameter per operand / result stablehlo.custom_call op, plus optional runtime-supplied parameters (attributes, contexts, etc.), returns xla::ffi::Error. eigh handler src/eigh.cpp, example, declared : first parameter input matrix; two Result<AnyBuffer> parameters output buffers (eigenvectors eigenvalues) XLA already allocated handler fills . return value Error::Success() success, one Error::Invalid… / Error::Internal factories surface runtime failure XLA.
code: #include "xla/ffi/api/ffi.h"   // ships in pjrt's inst/include/ using namespace xla::ffi;       // brings Error, AnyBuffer, Result<>, ... into scope  static Error do_eigh(AnyBuffer input,                      Result<AnyBuffer> v_out,                      Result<AnyBuffer> w_out);

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: C++ function yet usable MLIR – runtime way know do_eigh wants three buffers, order, attributes contexts. XLA_FFI_DEFINE_HANDLER macro attaches decoding metadata function emits symbol PJRT can call: pieces: eigh_handler – symbol name macro emits. (wrapped R external pointer) XLA_FFI_Handler* hand pjrt::pjrt_register_custom_call() R function. do_eigh – C++ function handler dispatches . Ffi::Bind().Arg<...>().Ret<...>()... – binding specification defines arguments passed do_eigh() execution. important builder methods, already saw : .Arg<AnyBuffer>() / .Ret<AnyBuffer>() – one operand / result dtype / rank. .Attrs<Dictionary>() – whole backend_config typed dictionary (used print_tensor). binding methods exist – statically typed Buffer<dtype, rank>, named .Attr<T>("name") individual backend_config entries, Token pure-ordering ops, RemainingArgs / RemainingRets variadic arity, additional Ctx<T> types including host ThreadPool (XLA’s intra-op pool, useful parallelising hand-rolled CPU work). full list see xla/ffi/api/ffi.h.
code: XLA_FFI_DEFINE_HANDLER(eigh_handler, do_eigh,                        Ffi::Bind()                            .Arg<AnyBuffer>()    // symmetric matrix                            .Ret<AnyBuffer>()    // eigenvectors (n, n)                            .Ret<AnyBuffer>());  // eigenvalues (n,)

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: {pjrt}’s build links LAPACK BLAS, CPU handler can call LAPACK / BLAS routine directly. LAPACK factorisation routines need working buffer whose optimal size depends input dimensions implementation-specific blocking parameters. standard idiom call routine twice: first workspace-size argument set -1, makes LAPACK skip actual work write optimal size first element workspace buffer; second time buffer size real factorisation. LAPACK signals success failure integer info -parameter (info = 0 success, anything else error). must checked every call – workspace query real call. {pjrt} provides small helper, lapack_check_info(info, routine_name) src/ffi_lapack.h, returns Error::Success() info == 0 Error::Internal("<routine_name> failed info = <info>") otherwise. Wrap LAPACK call PJRT_RETURN_IF_ERROR(lapack_check_info(info, "...")) XLA stops execution rather reading garbage results downstream. Windows pitfall: f32 LAPACK. R Windows uses bundled LAPACK, ships double-precision routines – single -precision available. handler calls single-precision LAPACK fail load Windows. macOS Linux unaffected (use system LAPACK, precisions). workaround promote f32 f64 around LAPACK call Windows; See existing linear algebra custom calls via C++ templates.

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: getting CUDA-specific handler details, note CUDA code pjrt structured differently “natural” CUDA backend. library based custom calls jaxlib, ships separate wheels CPU CUDA: jaxlib base CPU wheel, jax-cuda12-pjrt / jax-cuda12-plugin CUDA-specific wheels built --config=cuda CUDA toolkit build path. CUDA wheels can include CUDA SDK (Software Development Kit) headers, link CUDA libraries, use real types directly. R packages don’t equivalent mechanism. R CMD INSTALL builds one source tree one Makevars; “platform variant” way Python wheels CUDA extras. pjrt.binary load run machines without CUDA toolkit installed (even without NVIDIA GPU) – otherwise ’d break every CPU-user. pjrt’s CUDA path: never #includes CUDA SDK header, types every CUDA value opaque (void * streams handles, uintptr_t-aliased CUdeviceptr device addresses) don’t depend SDK’s typedefs compile time, maintains hand-rolled function-pointer table (CudaLibs ffi_cuda.h) cuSOLVER + CUDA driver entry points use, loads entry points lazily runtime via dlopen / dlsym inside get_cuda_libs() (ffi_cuda.cpp), package loads cleanly machines libcusolver.libcuda..1 aren’t present (loader just leaves table flagged unloaded, CUDA handlers return Error::Internal invoked). effect, CudaLibs + get_cuda_libs() job linker, dynamically hand – symbol lookup normal -lcusolver dependency system loader perform process startup, instead perform code runtime. trade-every cuSOLVER / driver function want use transcribed hand twice: typed function-pointer field CudaLibs (whose signature must match NVIDIA’s exactly – compiler can’t check us, since don’t include SDK headers tell expect), string symbol name passed dlsym loader. future, might move robust mechanism avoids redefinitions, e.g. including CUDA headers (might come licensing strings attached) e.g. generating declarations DSL.

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: CUDA concepts come every CUDA handler, briefly review . Host vs. device. CPU host GPU device. separate memory: host pointer can’t dereferenced device, device pointer can’t dereferenced host. Moving data requires explicit memcpy data can also directly copied device device. Streams. CUDA stream FIFO (first first ) queue operations device executes. Operations enqueued stream run order enqueued. Operations different streams can run concurrently dependency graph allows (e.g. memcpy one stream overlapping kernel another). Asynchronous execution. CUDA driver cuSOLVER calls – including *Async memcpys, kernel launches, cuSOLVER factorisations – non-blocking host. enqueue work stream return immediately, GPU actually done anything. host call’s return signals “now queued”; data isn’t real device gets point queue. source CUDA-handler pitfalls: read destination buffer right issuing async memcpy , ’ll see whatever , copy still queued. Stream synchronisation. cuStreamSynchronize(stream) blocks host every operation previously enqueued stream finished. ’s point “queued” turns “actually done” host’s perspective. Inside FFI handler, call cuStreamSynchronize unless genuinely need host-visible result – syncing forces host wait GPU breaks async pipelining XLA depends . place pjrt syncs today print_tensor’s CUDA path, copy data back host formatting . linalg handlers never sync: enqueue work stream return, XLA’s scheduling makes sure downstream op reads outputs runs kernel actually completed. Status codes. Every CUDA driver cuSOLVER call returns int status. 0 success; anything else error must propagated Error::Internal. dropped status doesn’t crash – leaves output buffer garbage surfaces much later wrong numerical answers, error message anywhere. PJRT_RETURN_IF_GPU_ERROR(expr, ) macro ffi_cuda.h wraps call site propagates non-zero statuses uniformly.

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: CUDA handler shape CPU one, two extra context parameters get bound front. src/eigh_cuda.cpp: differences CPU: void *stream – bound via Ctx<PlatformStream<void *>>(), CUDA stream XLA dispatched op . FFI exposes exactly one stream per invocation – API ask transfer stream – handler must enqueue device-side work : kernel launches, cuSOLVER calls, memcpy_dtod / memcpy_dtoh / memset_d8, synchronisation. ScratchAllocator &scratch – use device memory handler needs isn’t FFI input output: workspace library call, input copy, devInfo, etc. Allocations go XLA’s runtime allocator (one backs FFI input/output buffers, bytes visible XLA’s memory accounting) freed automatically handler returns – FFI binding’s destructor calls back runtime free every allocation made. Windows. handler symbol still defined Windows ([[Rcpp::export]] getter resolves), dispatcher returns Error(ErrorCode::kUnimplemented, ...) pjrt CUDA support Windows. See #ifdef _WIN32 guards src/eigh_cuda.cpp.
code: static Error eigh_cuda_impl(void *stream,                             ScratchAllocator &scratch,                             AnyBuffer input,                             Result<AnyBuffer> v_out,                             Result<AnyBuffer> w_out);  XLA_FFI_DEFINE_HANDLER(eigh_handler_cuda, do_eigh_cuda,                        Ffi::Bind()                            .Ctx<PlatformStream<void *>>()                            .Ctx<ScratchAllocator>()                            .Arg<AnyBuffer>()                            .Ret<AnyBuffer>()                            .Ret<AnyBuffer>());

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: cuSOLVER loaded lazily via dlopen (see CUDA intro ), CUDA handler wants call cuSOLVER goes CudaLibs function-pointer table Solver prologue defined ffi_cuda.h. kernel declares Solver solver(get_cuda_libs()); solver.begin(scratch, stream); invokes routines. begin() borrows stream-bound handle pool (see stateful-handle paragraph ) allocates devInfo int device memory. cuSOLVER/CUDA handles expensive create, cached process-wide singleton pool – map stream pointer free-list handles, protected mutex concurrent host-side borrow / return. kernel borrows handle stream’s free-list (creating one first time), uses , returns scope exit; handles live process reused across pjrt_execute calls. design adapted jaxlib. cuSOLVER factorisation routines follow query / allocate / run idiom LAPACK (covered CPU section), workspace query separate function _bufferSize suffix (cusolverDnSgeqrf_bufferSize, etc.) rather lwork = -1 like LAPACK. Note computing buffer size cheap host-side computation. cuSOLVER actually two status mechanisms, two different failure modes: return value every routine host-side int returned synchronously call comes back, indicating whether call accepted queued onto stream. always check PJRT_RETURN_IF_GPU_ERROR. devInfo int device-side int cuSOLVER’s kernel writes execution, using convention LAPACK’s info (0 = success, <0 = illegal argument position |info|, >0 = routine-specific). split exists cuSOLVER async stream: host return covers pre-launch failures, actual runtime / numerical status can reported kernel run, kernel needs somewhere device-side write . pass devInfo , don’t read , forces synchronization. , follow jaxlib decision.

what: Additional things to pay attention to
title: Adding Custom Calls
text: things aware implementing custom calls. Buffer aliasing. Stablehlo inherited XLA’s input-output aliasing. stablehlo.custom_call’s output_operand_aliases attribute lets XLA hand handler pointer input output. Routines overwrite input place read corrupted data. fix: copy input → destination buffer first, factor place , pointer-equality check copy skipped buffers already alias ((!= target) memcpy(...)). jaxlib uses pattern every linalg kernel; pjrt’s maybe_promote_inplace helper bakes guard CPU side. Dimension type mismatch byte-size overflow. Buffer dimensions arrive int64_t input.dimensions(), LAPACK cuSOLVER take int. naive cast wraps silently axes larger INT_MAX – use dim_to_int(dim, name, ) helper ffi_common.h, casts returns Error::InvalidArgument overflow. covers individual dimension, product: even dim_to_int widens m n int, expression like m * n * sizeof(T) still evaluates int arithmetic overflows large matrices (e.g. 50000 × 50000) result gets widened size_t. Cast least one operand size_t multiplication whole expression evaluates size_t: static_cast<size_t>(m) * n * sizeof(T). scratch.Allocate(0). ScratchAllocator::Allocate(0) returns std::nullopt, allocate_workspace<T> propagates Error::Internal. Downstream use custom calls prevent .

title: Get Started
text: {pjrt} package provides R interface PJRT, part OpenXLA project. PJRT portability layer allows frameworks work different hardware backends standardized interface. Anyone can implement PJRT plugin specific hardware backend, package currently supports CPU, NVIDIA GPU, Metal (Apple GPU; experimental) plugins. key design principle pjrt asynchronous dispatch: operations like buffer creation program execution return immediately PJRTBuffer objects may ready yet, allowing R continue preparing work device computes background. enables efficient overlap host device work, critical performance accelerators.

title: Get Started
text: creating plugin upon first use, shared library downloaded cached. plugin can used create client specific platform. Currently, exactly one client per platform stored global cache. client can one devices. relevant CPU clients, principle useful GPUs, although fully supported yet. important operations client supports data handling, compilation execution.
code: library(pjrt) plugin <- pjrt_plugin("cpu") plugin #> <PJRTPlugin:cpu> client <- plugin_client_create(plugin, "cpu") client #> <PJRTClient:cpu> the <- getFromNamespace("the", "pjrt") the[["clients"]][["cpu"]] #> <PJRTClient:cpu> the[["plugins"]][["cpu"]] #> <PJRTPlugin:cpu> cpu_devices <- devices(client) cpu_device <- cpu_devices[[1L]]

title: Get Started
text: Using client, can move data host (standard R objects) specific device create buffer. pjrt_buffer() returns PJRTBuffer immediately — buffer may ready yet (host--device transfer happens asynchronously), R block. Even transfer finishes, can inspect buffer metadata — operations non-blocking metadata available immediately: move data back host, use as_array(). blocks data available:
code: host_data <- 1:4 buf <- pjrt_buffer(host_data, device = cpu_device, shape = c(2L, 2L), dtype = "f32") buf #> PJRTBuffer  #>  1 3 #>  2 4 #> [ CPUf32{2x2} ] shape(buf) #> [1] 2 2 elt_type(buf) #> <f32> device(buf) #> <CpuDevice(id=0)> as_array(buf) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    1    3 #> [2,]    2    4

title: Get Started
text: PJRT compiles programs written HLO newer StableHLO device-specific executables. stablehlo package allows easily create StableHLO programs R. , define simple program adds two f32 tensors shape 2x2. First create program object, compile executable. Note compilation depends specific device.
code: src <- r"( func.func @main(   %x: tensor<2x2xf32>,   %y: tensor<2x2xf32> ) -> tensor<2x2xf32> {   %0 = "stablehlo.add"(%x, %y) : (tensor<2x2xf32>, tensor<2x2xf32>) -> tensor<2x2xf32>   "func.return"(%0): (tensor<2x2xf32>) -> () } )" program <- pjrt_program(src, format = "mlir") executable <- pjrt_compile(program, device = cpu_device) executable #> <PJRTLoadedExecutable>

title: Get Started
text: buffers executable ready, can run program. pjrt_execute() also returns PJRTBuffer immediately — R gets control back device computes background. Single outputs unpacked default; set simplify = FALSE always get list. retrieve result R array, call as_array():
code: x <- pjrt_buffer(c(1, 2, 3, 4), shape = c(2L, 2L), dtype = "f32") y <- pjrt_buffer(c(5, 6, 7, 8), shape = c(2L, 2L), dtype = "f32") result <- pjrt_execute(executable, x, y) result #> PJRTBuffer  #>   6 10 #>   8 12 #> [ CPUf32{2x2} ] as_array(result) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    6   10 #> [2,]    8   12

title: Get Started
text: running computations accelerators, synchronous approach creates synchronization bubbles — periods either host device sits idle waiting : async dispatch, host device can work parallel: pjrt, happens automatically. pjrt_buffer() pjrt_execute() return PJRTBuffer objects immediately without blocking R. buffer may ready yet, can pass directly operations. pay synchronization cost actually need host-side result (e.g. calling as_array()).
code: Host:   [prepare data] [wait...] [prepare data] [wait...] [prepare data] Device: [wait.........] [compute] [wait.........] [compute] [wait.........] Host:   [prepare batch 1] [prepare batch 2] [prepare batch 3] [prepare batch 4] Device:                   [compute 1......] [compute 2......] [compute 3......]

title: Get Started
text: PJRTBuffer transparent async type — can used directly operations even underlying computation hasn’t completed yet. PJRT handles dependencies internally. PJRTArrayPromise represents data transit device host. R arrays plain R objects (control like PJRTBuffer), need separate promise type represent “array isn’t ready yet.” two functions synchronization: await(x) — block PJRTBuffer ready device. Pure synchronization; data leaves device. Useful timing error checking, required passing buffer pjrt_execute(). value(x) — block PJRTArrayPromise completes, materialize return R array. get data device. types also support is_ready(x) non-blocking readiness checks.

title: Get Started
text: Buffers can passed directly operations without waiting — PJRT handles dependencies internally: can also chain execution async device--host transfer:
code: # Both transfers start immediately buf1 <- pjrt_buffer(matrix(1:4, 2, 2), dtype = "f32") buf2 <- pjrt_buffer(matrix(5:8, 2, 2), dtype = "f32")  # Execute with promise inputs — no need to wait for transfers result <- pjrt_execute(executable, buf1, buf2)  # Only block when we need the R array as_array(result) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    6   10 #> [2,]    8   12 src2 <- r"( func.func @main(%x: tensor<1000x1000xf32>) -> tensor<1000x1000xf32> {   %0 = "stablehlo.add"(%x, %x) : (tensor<1000x1000xf32>, tensor<1000x1000xf32>) -> tensor<1000x1000xf32>   "func.return"(%0): (tensor<1000x1000xf32>) -> () } )" exec2 <- pjrt_compile(pjrt_program(src2))  # Full async pipeline: transfer -> execute -> transfer back buf <- pjrt_buffer(matrix(runif(1e6), 1000, 1000), dtype = "f32") result <- pjrt_execute(exec2, buf) async_output <- as_array_async(result)   # returns immediately  # R can do other work here...  # Block only when we need the final R array output <- value(async_output) dim(output) #> [1] 1000 1000

title: Get Started
text: Blocking occurs : Calling await() buffer Calling as_array() buffer Calling value() PJRTArrayPromise Printing buffer (needs read values display ) Operations like shape(), elt_type(), device() block — buffer metadata available immediately.

title: Get Started
text: Bad — blocking every iteration: Better — log previous iteration’s metrics device computes next:
code: for (i in seq_len(n_iterations)) {   result <- pjrt_execute(executable, input)   metrics <- as_array(result)  # blocks! device idles during transfer   cat("Step", i, "loss:", metrics[1], "\n")   input <- prepare_next_batch()  # device still idle } prev_result <- NULL for (i in seq_len(n_iterations)) {   result <- pjrt_execute(executable, input)    if (!is.null(prev_result)) {     metrics <- as_array(prev_result)  # previous result likely ready     cat("Step", i - 1, "loss:", metrics[1], "\n")   }    input <- prepare_next_batch()  # overlap with device work   prev_result <- result }

title: Get Started
code: # Multiply-add program src3 <- r"( func.func @main(%x: tensor<100x100xf32>, %y: tensor<100x100xf32>) -> tensor<100x100xf32> {   %0 = "stablehlo.multiply"(%x, %y) : (tensor<100x100xf32>, tensor<100x100xf32>) -> tensor<100x100xf32>   %1 = "stablehlo.add"(%0, %x) : (tensor<100x100xf32>, tensor<100x100xf32>) -> tensor<100x100xf32>   "func.return"(%1): (tensor<100x100xf32>) -> () } )" exec3 <- pjrt_compile(pjrt_program(src3))  # Mini computation loop with chaining shp <- c(100L, 100L) x <- pjrt_buffer(matrix(runif(prod(shp)), shp[1], shp[2]), dtype = "f32") y <- pjrt_buffer(matrix(runif(prod(shp)), shp[1], shp[2]), dtype = "f32")  for (step in seq_len(5)) {   # Returns immediately — auto-waits for x and y if needed   result <- pjrt_execute(exec3, x, y)   # Chain: use result as input to next iteration   x <- result }  # Only retrieve the final value final <- as_array(result) cat("Final result shape:", dim(final), "\n") #> Final result shape: 100 100 cat("Final result[1,1]:", final[1, 1], "\n") #> Final result[1,1]: 22.14824

title: Get Started
text: support reading writing buffers using safetensors format, allows storing named lists buffers.

title: Get Started
text: async dispatch design pjrt inspired JAX’s approach: Asynchronous dispatch JAX Training Cookbook: Efficiency via Asynchronous Dispatch

code: @Manual{,   title = {pjrt: R Interface to PJRT},   author = {Sebastian Fischer and Daniel Falbel},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.4.0.9000},   url = {https://r-xla.github.io/pjrt/}, }

title: R Interface to PJRT
text: Package website: release | dev {pjrt} package provides R interface PJRT (Pluggable Jit RunTime), allows run XLA stableHLO programs various hardware backends. programs framework hardware agnostic, means can generated ML frameworks jax, run PJRT specified backend (CPU, GPU, etc.). low-level R interface create stableHLO programs, see stablehlo package.

title: R Interface to PJRT
text: GitHub: can also install r-universe, adding code .Rprofile.
code: pak::pak("r-xla/pjrt") options(repos = c(   rxla = "https://r-xla.r-universe.dev",   CRAN = "https://cloud.r-project.org/" ))

title: R Interface to PJRT
text: use CUDA backend, install {cuda12.8} R package provides required CUDA runtime libraries need compatible CUDA driver. Alternatively, install r-universe. {cuda12.8} package installed, correct runtime libraries need installed system, can difficult set . specific versions CUDA runtime libraries provided {cuda12.8} provided . Troubleshooting trouble-shoot CUDA installation, run following new R session maximum debug output. Note another package using different cudatoolkit package (e.g. using {torch}), might issues, case ’s best run separate R processes.
code: pak::pak("mlverse/cudatoolkit/cuda12.8") install.packages("cuda12.8", repos = "https://mlverse.r-universe.dev") Sys.setenv(PJRT_DEBUG = "1", TF_CPP_MIN_LOG_LEVEL = "0") pjrt::pjrt_buffer(1, device = "cuda")

title: R Interface to PJRT
text: , create run stableHLO program adds two f32 tensors shape (2, 2).
code: library(pjrt) src <- r"( func.func @main(   %x: tensor<2x2xf32>,   %y: tensor<2x2xf32> ) -> tensor<2x2xf32> {   %0 = "stablehlo.add"(%x, %y) : (tensor<2x2xf32>, tensor<2x2xf32>) -> tensor<2x2xf32>   "func.return"(%0): (tensor<2x2xf32>) -> () } )" program <- pjrt_program(src, format = "mlir") program #> PJRTProgram(format=mlir, code_size=221) #>  #> func.func @main( #>   %x: tensor<2x2xf32>, #>   %y: tensor<2x2xf32> #> ) -> tensor<2x2xf32> { #> ... executable <- pjrt_compile(program, device = "cpu")  x <- pjrt_buffer(c(1, 2, 3, 4), shape = c(2, 2), dtype = "f32") x #> PJRTBuffer  #>  1 3 #>  2 4 #> [ CPUf32{2x2} ] y <- pjrt_buffer(c(5, 6, 7, 8), shape = c(2, 2), dtype = "f32") y #> PJRTBuffer  #>  5 7 #>  6 8 #> [ CPUf32{2x2} ]  pjrt_execute(executable, x, y) #> PJRTBuffer  #>   6 10 #>   8 12 #> [ CPUf32{2x2} ]

title: R Interface to PJRT
text: Compile stableHLO programs hardware-specific executables. Provide runtime execute compiled programs. Convert buffers R arrays vectors. Read write buffers using safetensors format. Operations executed asynchronously.

title: R Interface to PJRT
text: ✅ CPU backend fully supported. ✅ CUDA (NVIDIA GPU) backend fully supported. ✅ CPU backend fully supported. ❌ GPU supported. ✅ CPU backend fully supported. ⚠️ GPU supported via Windows Subsystem Linux (WSL2). ✅ CPU backend supported. ⚠️ Metal (Apple GPU) backend available fully functional.

title: R Interface to PJRT
text: development package supported MaRDI. Without OpenXLA, none possible. design {pjrt} package inspired gopjrt implementation. PJRT C API. PJRT C API FFI Extension. Various protobuf files, see ./tools/copy-proto.R ones. Plugin implementations CPU CUDA (using builds zml/pjrt-artifacts). Metal, using plugin implementation jax-metal.

title: Convert a PJRTBuffer to an R Array — as_array.PJRTBuffer
text: x (PJRTBuffer) Buffer convert. check (logical(1)) TRUE, sanity-check materialized R vector losing information across device--host boundary, abort problematic value detected: i32 / i64: NA result. R's NA_integer_ shares bit pattern INT_MIN; bit64's NA_integer64_ shares INT64_MIN. legitimate device value bit patterns indistinguishable NA materialized R. ui64: negative value result. ui64 stored bit64::integer64 (signed 64-bit), wraps values >= 2^63 negative — exactly 2^63 becomes NA_integer64_, anything becomes non-NA negative integer64. -op float, boolean, small/unsigned-32 integer dtypes — ui32 now stored integer64 full headroom, produce wrapped NA value. ... Additional arguments (unused).

title: Convert a PJRTBuffer to an R Array — as_array.PJRTBuffer
text: R array (vector shape integer()).

what: Convert buffer to R array asynchronously — as_array_async
title: Convert buffer to R array asynchronously — as_array_async
text: Start asynchronous transfer buffer data device host. Returns immediately PJRTArrayPromise object. Use value() get R array (blocks ready). Use is_ready() check transfer completed (non-blocking).

code: as_array_async(x, ...)

title: Convert buffer to R array asynchronously — as_array_async

title: Convert buffer to R array asynchronously — as_array_async
code: buf <- pjrt_buffer(c(1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0), shape = c(2, 2), dtype = "f32") result <- as_array_async(buf) is_ready(result) #> [1] TRUE value(result) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    1    3 #> [2,]    2    4

what: pjrt: R Interface to PJRT — pjrt-package
title: pjrt: R Interface to PJRT — pjrt-package
text: Provides R interface PJRT (Pluggable Jit RunTime), allows run XLA stableHLO programs variety hardware backends including CPU, GPU, TPU.

title: pjrt: R Interface to PJRT — pjrt-package
text: Configuration options provided XLA XLA provides various configuration options, documentation scattered across various websites. options include: TF_CPP_MIN_LOG_LEVEL: Logging level PJRT C++ API: 0: shows info, warnings errors 1: shows warnings errors 2: shows errors 3: shows nothing XLA_FLAGS: See openxla website information. Configuration options provided package PJRT_PLATFORM: Default platform use, falls back "cpu". PJRT_PLUGIN_PATH_<PLATFORM>: Path custom plugin library file specific platform (e.g., PJRT_PLUGIN_PATH_CPU, PJRT_PLUGIN_PATH_CUDA, PJRT_PLUGIN_PATH_METAL). set, package use path instead downloading plugin. PJRT_PLUGIN_URL_<PLATFORM>: URL download plugin specific platform (e.g., PJRT_PLUGIN_URL_CPU, PJRT_PLUGIN_URL_CUDA, PJRT_PLUGIN_URL_METAL). set, overrides default plugin download URL. PJRT_ZML_ARTIFACT_VERSION: Version ZML artifacts download. used downloading plugins zml/pjrt-artifacts. PJRT_CPU_DEVICE_COUNT: number CPU devices use. Defaults 1. primarily intended testing purposes. PJRT_CUDA_R_PACKAGE: Name R package providing CUDA libraries. Defaults value cuda12.8. Set use different CUDA toolkit package, note versions may work XLA plugin. PJRT_DEBUG: set (non-empty value), enables verbose debug output via cli::cli_inform().

title: pjrt: R Interface to PJRT — pjrt-package
text: pjrt package MIT-licensed. CUDA backend dynamically loads NVIDIA software bundled pjrt, downloaded NVIDIA's official redistributable channels CUDA toolkit R package (e.g. cuda12.8) install time. use governed NVIDIA CUDA Toolkit EULA, exception cuDNN, covered NVIDIA cuDNN SLA, NCCL, covered license. installing using CUDA backend accept terms.

title: pjrt: R Interface to PJRT — pjrt-package
text: Maintainer: Sebastian Fischer seb.fischer@tutamail.com (ORCID) Authors: Daniel Falbel daniel@posit.co (ORCID)

title: Create a PJRT Buffer — pjrt_buffer
text: Create PJRT Buffer R object. numeric PJRT buffer array 0-dimensional arrays used scalars. pjrt_buffer create array dimensions (1) vector length 1, pjrt_scalar create 0-dimensional array R vector length 1. create empty buffer (least one dimension must 0), use pjrt_empty. Important: checks performed creating buffer, need ensure data fits selected element type (e.g., prevent buffer overflow) NA values present.

title: Create a PJRT Buffer — pjrt_buffer
code: pjrt_buffer(   data,   dtype = NULL,   device = NULL,   shape = NULL,   check = FALSE,   ... )  pjrt_scalar(data, dtype = NULL, device = NULL, check = FALSE, ...)  pjrt_empty(dtype, shape, device = NULL)

title: Create a PJRT Buffer — pjrt_buffer
text: data () Data convert PJRTBuffer. dtype (NULL | character(1) | DataType) type buffer. Currently supported types : "pred": predicate (.e. boolean) "{s,u}{8,16,32,64}": Signed unsigned integer (integer data). "f{32,64}": Floating point (double integer data). default (NULL) depends method: logical -> "pred" integer -> "i32" double -> "f32" raw -> must supplied device (NULL | PJRTDevice | character(1)) PJRTDevice object name platform use ("cpu", "cuda", ...), case first device platform used. default use CPU platform, can configured via PJRT_PLATFORM environment variable. shape (NULL | integer()) dimensions buffer. default (NULL) infer data possible. default (NULL) depends method. check (logical(1)) TRUE, scan data NA values transferring device raise error present. R's NA markers representation XLA level (e.g. NA_integer_ just bit pattern -2147483648, NA logical type silently coerced TRUE), missing values silently lost transfer. Defaults FALSE performance; set TRUE fail loudly instead silently corrupting data. applicable raw input. ... () Additional arguments. raw types, includes: row_major: Whether read data row-major format column-major format. R uses column-major format.

title: Create a PJRT Buffer — pjrt_buffer
text: PJRTBuffer

title: Create a PJRT Buffer — pjrt_buffer
text: platform() -> character(1): platform name buffer ("cpu", "cuda", ...). device() -> PJRTDevice: device buffer (also includes device number) elt_type() -> PJRTElementType: element type buffer. shape() -> integer(): shape buffer.

title: Create a PJRT Buffer — pjrt_buffer
text: as_array() -> array | vector: converting back R (vector used shape integer()). as_raw() -> raw raw vector.

title: Create a PJRT Buffer — pjrt_buffer
text: safetensors::safe_save_file writing safetensors file. safetensors::safe_load_file reading safetensors file.

title: Create a PJRT Buffer — pjrt_buffer
text: calling function vector length 1, resulting shape 1L. create 0-dimensional buffer, use pjrt_scalar resulting shape integer().

title: Create a PJRT Buffer — pjrt_buffer
code: # Create a buffer from a numeric vector buf <- pjrt_buffer(c(1, 2, 3, 4)) buf #> PJRTBuffer  #>  1 #>  2 #>  3 #>  4 #> [ CPUf32{4} ]   # Create a buffer from a matrix mat <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2) buf <- pjrt_buffer(mat) buf #> PJRTBuffer  #>  1 3 5 #>  2 4 6 #> [ CPUi32{2x3} ]   # Create an integer buffer from an array arr <- array(1:8, dim = c(2, 2, 2)) buf <- pjrt_buffer(arr) # Create a scalar (0-dimensional array) scalar <- pjrt_scalar(42, dtype = "f32") scalar #> PJRTBuffer  #>  42 #> [ CPUf32{} ]  # Create an empty buffer empty <- pjrt_empty(dtype = "f32", shape = c(0, 3)) empty #> PJRTBuffer  #> [ CPUf32{0x3} ]

code: # Create a simple program src <- r"( func.func @main(\%arg0: tensor<2xf32>) -> tensor<2xf32> {   return \%arg0 : tensor<2xf32> } )" prog <- pjrt_program(src = src) exec <- pjrt_compile(prog) #> Error: -:2:17: error: unexpected character #> <unknown>:0: error: Failed to parse using StableHLO v1.13.2, this could indicate forward incompatibility, >12w old unsupported plugin, or a portable artifact that needs to be further downgraded.

title: Execute a PJRT program — pjrt_execute
text: Execute PJRT program given inputs execution options. Returns immediately PJRTBuffer object(s) may ready yet. Important: Arguments passed position names ignored. Inputs can PJRTBuffer objects, including buffers yet ready. PJRT handles dependencies internally. Use await() block result ready. Use is_ready() check execution completed (non-blocking). Use as_array_async() chain async buffer--host transfer.

title: Execute a PJRT program — pjrt_execute
code: pjrt_execute(executable, ..., execution_options = NULL, simplify = TRUE)

title: Execute a PJRT program — pjrt_execute
text: executable (PJRTLoadedExecutable) PJRT program ... (PJRTBuffer) Inputs program. Named ignored arguments passed order. execution_options (PJRTExecuteOptions) Optional execution options configuring buffer donation settings. simplify (logical(1)) TRUE (default), single output returned PJRTBuffer. FALSE, single output returned list length 1 containing PJRTBuffer.

title: Execute a PJRT program — pjrt_execute
text: PJRTBuffer | list PJRTBuffers

title: Execute a PJRT program — pjrt_execute
code: # Create and compile a simple identity program src <- r"( func.func @main(   \%x: tensor<2x2xf32>,   \%y: tensor<2x2xf32> ) -> tensor<2x2xf32> {   \%0 = "stablehlo.add"(\%x, \%y) : (tensor<2x2xf32>, tensor<2x2xf32>) -> tensor<2x2xf32>   "func.return"(\%0): (tensor<2x2xf32>) -> () } )" prog <- pjrt_program(src = src) exec <- pjrt_compile(prog) #> Error: -:3:3: error: unexpected character #> <unknown>:0: error: Failed to parse using StableHLO v1.13.2, this could indicate forward incompatibility, >12w old unsupported plugin, or a portable artifact that needs to be further downgraded.  # Execute with input x <- pjrt_buffer(c(1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0), shape = c(2, 2), dtype = "f32") y <- pjrt_buffer(c(5, 6, 7, 8), shape = c(2, 2), dtype = "f32") pjrt_execute(exec, x, y) #> Error: object 'exec' not found

title: Create Execution Options — pjrt_execution_options
text: Create execution options configuring PJRT program executed, including buffer donation settings. Important: enough mark buffer donatable (donatable) runtime. program also needs specify compile-time via input-output aliasing (stableHLO attribute tf.aliasing_output).

title: Create Execution Options — pjrt_execution_options
code: pjrt_execution_options(non_donatable_input_indices = integer(), launch_id = 0L)

title: Create Execution Options — pjrt_execution_options
text: non_donatable_input_indices (integer()) vector input buffer indices donated execution (0-based). Buffer donation allows runtime reuse input buffers outputs possible, can improve performance. However, input buffer referenced multiple times needs preserved, marked non-donatable. launch_id (integer(1)) optional launch identifier multi-device executions. can used detect scheduling errors multi-host programs.

title: Create Execution Options — pjrt_execution_options
text: PJRTExecuteOptions

title: Create Execution Options — pjrt_execution_options
code: # Create default execution options opts <- pjrt_execution_options()  # Mark buffer 0 as non-donatable opts <- pjrt_execution_options(non_donatable_input_indices = 0L)

what: Register a Custom Call Handler — pjrt_register_custom_call
title: Register a Custom Call Handler — pjrt_register_custom_call
text: Register XLA FFI handler use stablehlo.custom_call. Handlers C/C++ functions defined using XLA FFI API (see xla/ffi/api/ffi.h shipped pjrt's inst/include/). passed function external pointers. Registration deferred: PJRT plugin given platform yet loaded, handler queued registered automatically pjrt_plugin() loads .

title: Register a Custom Call Handler — pjrt_register_custom_call
code: pjrt_register_custom_call(target_name, handler, .package = NULL)

title: Register a Custom Call Handler — pjrt_register_custom_call
text: target_name (character(1)) target name used stablehlo.custom_call @target_name(...). handler named list external pointers (externalptr) XLA_FFI_Handlers, keyed PJRT platform name (e.g., list(host = ptr) list(host = cpu_ptr, cuda = cuda_ptr)). .package (character(1) NULL) package registering handler. provided, handlers automatically removed registry package unloads.

title: Register a Custom Call Handler — pjrt_register_custom_call
text: NULL (invisibly).

title: Print a PJRT Buffer — print.PJRTBuffer
text: Print PJRTBuffer.

title: Print a PJRT Buffer — print.PJRTBuffer
code: # S3 method for class 'PJRTBuffer' print(   x,   max_rows = getOption("pjrt.print_max_rows", 30L),   max_width = getOption("pjrt.print_max_width", 85L),   max_rows_slice = getOption("pjrt.print_max_rows_slice", max_rows),   header = TRUE,   footer = NULL,   ... )

title: Print a PJRT Buffer — print.PJRTBuffer
text: x (PJRTBuffer) buffer. max_rows (integer(1)) maximum number rows print, excluding header footer. max_width (integer(1)) maximum width (characters) printed buffer. Set negative values limit. Note small values, actual printed width might slightly smaller least one column printed. Also, limit affects printed rows containing actual data, rows might exceed width. max_rows_slice (integer(1)) maximum number rows print slice. header (logical(1)) Whether print header. footer (NULL character(1)) footer line print. NULL (default), prints standard [ <PLATFORM><TYPE>{<SHAPE>} ] summary. Use "" suppress. ... Additional arguments (unused).

title: pjrt 0.4.0
text: Added QR, LU, SVD, symmetric eigendecomposition support CPU CUDA via FFI registration mechanism. Added vignette register custom calls via FFI registration mechanisms coverage CUDA CPU-specific aspects. Added support bit64 package better support long integers. pjrt_buffer(), pjrt_scalar(), as_array() gain check argument (default FALSE). TRUE, call errors instead silently losing information: input data contains NAs, output materialized R vector contains value ’s indistinguishable NA wrapped integer container. as_array() ui32 buffer now returns bit64::integer64 instead base integer, values >= 2^31 round-trip losslessly rather wrapping negative.

title: pjrt 0.3.0
text: Added buffer_copy() function copy buffer devices. New pjrt_register_custom_call() allows external packages register C/C++ XLA FFI handlers PJRT plugin. Registration deferred plugin loads, handlers can registered .onLoad(). pjrt_device() now returns cached PJRTDevice instances, repeated calls device yield objects stable identity (useful hashing caching, e.g. {anvil}’s JIT).

title: pjrt 0.3.0
text: configure script now uses protoc compiler installation linked protobuf library, preventing version mismatches multiple protobuf versions installed. Compiling program specific CPU device (e.g. cpu:1) now targets device instead silently falling back cpu:0. Fixed device targeting compiling distributed PJRT client, global device IDs local hardware ordinals diverge.

title: pjrt 0.3.0
text: Improved error message attempting use CUDA unsupported OS platform.

title: pjrt 0.2.0
text: Operations host <-> device transfers program execution previously synchronous. Now, asynchronous considerable performance benefits, especially GPU. Specifically: pjrt_buffer() pjrt_execute() return immediately, returned buffer necessarily ready. await transfer computation buffer, use await(). However, handled within PJRT, function never called user. as_array() still synchronous, now asynchronous version as_array_async() rarely needed. used, returns PJRTArrayPromise object can converted R array/vector via value(). check whether PJRTBuffer PJRTArrayPromise ready, use is_ready().

title: pjrt 0.2.0
text: Added dtype support PJRTBuffers via tengen::dtype S3 generic. "bool" now accepted alias "i1"/"pred". Accept DataType objects dtype parameter pjrt_buffer(). Support device argument pjrt_compile().

title: pjrt 0.2.0
text: Protect segfaults raw buffer conversion. Protect segfault device mismatch pjrt_execute().

title: pjrt 0.2.0
text: Added support Linux ARM (aarch64) using CPU backend. Simplified CUDA installation via cuda12.8 package, now requires compatible drivers installed.

title: pjrt 0.2.0
text: printer PJRTBuffer now uses "bool" instead "pred" avoid discrepancies {anvl}.

title: pjrt 0.1.1
text: Fix formatting +-Inf/NaN f64

title: pjrt 0.1.0
text: Initial release
